1.blurb: Mesopotamia's story starts on a flat alluvial plain between two rivers — the Tigris and the Euphrates — in a climate that was already hot and drying out 10,000 years ago. No loess plateau you can dig with a stick, no rice paddies along a marshy delta. Just an empty floodplain you had to irrigate to farm, which turned out to be exactly the pressure that pushed people into the world's first cities.

The closest parallel to Neolithic China is also the strangest civilization in the ancient world: the Indus was so completely forgotten after its decline that no later Indian text even mentions it by name. It took a 19th-century British railway crew pilfering ancient bricks for track ballast to accidentally rediscover it in the 1850s.

1.matchText: farther east in modern Pakistan, is the closest parallel
1.blurb: There is a second close parallel, less famous than the Indus: the Kerma kingdom of Upper Nubia (~2500–1500 BCE). Kerma was a full-fledged African urban state with monumental architecture, royal burials, and a thousand-year political history, and it left no writing of its own that we can read. Its story, like early China's, has to be reconstructed from archaeology against the biased outside observation of a literate neighbor — in Kerma's case, Egypt.

3.blurb: While China was still painting pottery and building moated villages, Mesopotamia had already built the world's first real city. Uruk, down on the Euphrates, had tens of thousands of people, a giant temple complex, and was on the verge of inventing writing — something the Chinese wouldn't match for another 1,500 years.

At almost the exact moment China was domesticating the silkworm, the villages of the Indus foothills were domesticating cotton — the other great Asian fiber. Both would eventually travel the world, but in 3500 BCE they were still local secrets, made in two civilizations that didn't yet know the other existed.

5.blurb: The Akkadian Empire is the first multi-ethnic empire in human history — Sargon of Akkad stitched together the Sumerian city-states under a single king around 2334 BCE and ran the thing for roughly 150 years before the same 4.2 kiloyear drought that may have drowned Liangzhu also finished off Akkad. Two ends of Eurasia, one climate event, two collapsed civilizations.

The Indus Valley's decline sits right in the same climate window as Liangzhu's. No single catastrophic event — more of a long unravelling: the great cities lost population, the standardized weights stopped being made, the script disappeared. By 1700 BCE Mohenjo-daro was a ghost town. The 4.2ky drought gets blamed for a lot, and the Indus is one of its clearest casualties.

6.blurb: Uruk's legendary walls were supposedly built by Gilgamesh himself — the hero-king of the world's oldest surviving epic. They enclosed roughly 6 square kilometers of city, which made Uruk the biggest urban place on Earth at the time. The same era saw Mesopotamia splinter into competing city-states that fought each other constantly — which is almost exactly the situation the Longshan walled towns were in, at the other end of Asia, a few centuries later.

6.matchText: Kot Diji's defensive walls by 2800 BCE
6.blurb: Kot Diji was one of the Early Harappan towns that rehearsed everything the Mature Indus cities would later perfect — standardized mud bricks, grid streets, a citadel mound, and big mud-brick defensive walls by 2800 BCE. The Indus was already building fortified towns while Longshan China was still working up to the same idea.

When Leonard Woolley dug the Royal Tombs of Ur in the 1920s, he found something out of a nightmare: whole rows of attendants — musicians, soldiers, ladies-in-waiting — laid out around their dead ruler, apparently drugged and then killed to accompany them into the afterlife. Queen Puabi's tomb alone contained dozens of sacrificed retainers. At almost the same moment, Shimao's Longshan rulers were burying rows of decapitated young women in their city walls. Same idea, opposite ends of Eurasia.

The Indus ran one of the most impressive long-distance trade networks of the Bronze Age. Lapis lazuli from the mines of Badakhshan in modern Afghanistan flowed west through an Indus trading outpost called Shortughai — placed specifically to control that flow — and ended up in Sumerian temples 1,500 miles away. At the same time, jade was running east across China in the opposite direction. Two Bronze Age luxury-stone networks, same continent, not quite touching yet.

6.matchText: Han dynasty and the Silk Road
6.blurb: When the Han dynasty finally opened the Silk Road a thousand years after this jade trade, its western terminus was the Achaemenid Persian Empire's successor states — the same imperial state that Cyrus the Great had built directly on the Elamite bureaucratic tradition, running in Elamite cuneiform out of Persepolis, with royal legitimacy still tracing through the old 'king of Anshan' title all the way back to the Elamite highland capital. The Silk Road's Persian endpoint was Elam's continuation by another name.

Sumerian astronomers were already tracking the planets and moon phases by 2300 BCE — but they were doing it in cuneiform texts, not in architecture. Taosi is running roughly the same astronomical program at the same moment, but encoding the results in a physical observatory built of rammed earth. Same science, completely different media.

The comparison extends further than Ur. At Kerma in Upper Nubia, the largest royal tumuli contained three to four hundred sacrificed retainers — an order of magnitude larger than the 74 attendants in Queen Puabi's death pit at Ur. Of the four Bronze Age cradles that left substantial royal burial grounds (Ur, Shimao, Kerma, Indus Valley), three practiced retainer sacrifice at different scales and the Indus Valley alone did not.

6.matchText: Of the major Bronze Age cradles, it is the Indus and the Andes that are the outliers
6.blurb: Adding the Kerma kingdom as a fourth cradle makes the picture sharper. Kerma's royal tumuli contained hundreds of sacrificed retainers per king — larger than Ur's and Shimao's combined. Three of the four Bronze Age cradles practiced large-scale royal retainer sacrifice. The Indus Valley, alone, did not.
Longshan's walled, warring towns are China's threshold of statehood — the same village-to-polity jump Caral made in Peru, independently.
Longshan chiefs hoarding jade and waging war is the Chinese version of the Uruk-to-dynasty transition Mesopotamia ran two millennia earlier.

7.blurb: The Akkadian Empire — Sargon and his dynasty, the first multi-ethnic empire in human history — collapsed right around 2150 BCE, smack in the middle of the 4.2 kiloyear drought. The same climate event that broke Akkad broke the Indus and, a couple of centuries later, produced the Yellow River flood that Chinese memory would attach to Yu the Great. One global disaster, three different civilizational echoes.

Ziusudra is the Sumerian Noah — a righteous king warned by a god to build a giant boat and survive a flood sent to destroy humanity. His story, written down on a Sumerian clay tablet around 1600 BCE, is the oldest flood myth on record, and it's almost certainly the ancestor of the biblical Noah story. The Chinese Yu-the-Great flood, the Mesopotamian Ziusudra flood, and the Indus Valley's material collapse may all be distorted memories of the same global 4.2ky climate catastrophe.
The Yu-the-Great flood myth encodes a real environmental memory — like the Indus, early Chinese civilization was a negotiation with a violent, shifting river.
The Xia 'first dynasty' is China's Sumer — half-legendary, archaeologically blurry, but the template every later dynasty claimed to inherit.

8.blurb: The Dancing Girl is a 4-inch bronze figurine from Mohenjo-daro — a slim young woman with her hand on her hip and a stack of bangles up one arm, looking remarkably like she's about to say something cutting. She was cast using the lost-wax method around 2500 BCE, about 600 years before Erlitou started casting any bronze at all. The Indus got to metal casting first; China got there later but used a completely different technique.

8.matchText: Mesopotamia had been in the Bronze Age since ~3300 BCE
8.blurb: Mesopotamia started alloying copper and tin into bronze around 3300 BCE — about 1,500 years before Erlitou did the same thing. Not because the Chinese were slower thinkers, but because Mesopotamia sat on the end of the Iranian plateau trade routes that brought tin in from mountain sources, while China's tin had to come from much farther south and west. Geography set the schedule.

Mesopotamia is the anti-China: same river valley, totally different people cycling through every few centuries. Sumerians, Akkadians, Babylonians, Assyrians, Chaldeans, Persians, Greeks, Arabs — each of them inherited the previous civilization's cities, canals, and gods at the same physical addresses, but none of them remembered themselves as a continuation of the last. The land stayed; the people kept being replaced.

The Indus is the other extreme. After the Mature Harappan cities declined around 1900 BCE, the civilization was so thoroughly forgotten that no later Indian text — not the Vedas, not the epics, not the Buddhist canon — mentions it by name. Nobody even knew it had existed until the 1850s, when a British railway engineer started noticing that the bricks his crew was stealing from an old mound were suspiciously ancient. China remembered everything. The Indus remembered nothing.

8.matchText: Sumerians, Akkadians, Babylonians, Assyrians, Chaldeans, Persians
8.blurb: The 'Persians' at the end of that succession list weren't Iranian newcomers replacing the Semitic imperial tradition. In Cyrus the Great's own Cylinder, the Achaemenid royal line traces itself through four generations of 'king of Anshan' titles — Anshan being the Elamite highland capital in Fars — and the 30,000 tablets of the Persepolis Fortification Archive that recorded the Persian Empire's day-to-day operations are in Elamite cuneiform, not Persian. The Persian Empire was Elam's final form, running the bureaucracy of the largest ancient state in history in a dead kingdom's scribal language.
Erlitou — palace platforms, bronze foundries, no writing yet — is China's Bronze Age dawn, structurally like the Indus cities a thousand years before.
Erlitou turns legend into stratigraphy the way Uruk did for Mesopotamia: the first city you can stand in, not just read about.

2.blurb: China's twin millet/rice cradles parallel the Andes' own multi-zone agricultural origin — complex society bootstrapping from domesticated staples on opposite sides of the planet.
